original: Battle of Crannon between Athens and Macedon following the death of Alexander the Great
Battle at Constantinople: Slavic, Persians and Avar siege of Constantinople fails, keeping the Byzantine Empire intact
Forces from the Mayan city of Palenque, under its leader Pakal, defeat nearby Santa Elena and take its ruler captive
Stephen III [IV] begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Otto I the Great crowned King of Germany (East Francia)
Council of Pisa, an attempt to end the Western Schism in the Catholic Church with two opposing popes, closes after electing a third, Alexander V
Valais witch trial proceedings begin in Valais Canton, Switzerland, the first organized witch trials
Ming Dynasty Chinese military general Cao Qin stages a coup against the Tianshun Emperor
Spanish troops conquer and plunder Oudewater in the Netherlands during the Dutch revolt, killing almost all its inhabitants
Sidney, 1st Earl of Godolphin, resigns as English Minister of Finance
Battle of Gangut: Russian Navy wins its first major victory during the Great Northern War against Sweden
Slave uprising in Curaçao
Fort Loudoun, Tennessee, surrenders to Cherokee Indians
US Congress creates the Department of War and the United States Lighthouse Establishment
Battle of Boyacá: Bolívar defeats the Spanish in Colombia
First potatoes are planted in Hawaii
Battle of Moorefield, West Virginia: Surprise Union raid kills 400 Confederates and captures 400 horses, badly damaging Confederate cavalry in the Shenandoah Valley
Opening of the Openshaw Citadel, the "Poor Man's Palace," by the Salvation Army in Manchester
Hatfields of southwest West Virginia and McCoys of eastern Kentucky feud, with 100 wounded or dead
Germany annexes Angra Pequena / German South West Africa (modern Namibia)
Five German warships anchor off Zanzibar
Theophilus Van Kannel of Philadelphia patents revolving door
53rd US Congress (1893-95) convenes with a continued Democratic majority
New Jersey fishermen George Harbo and Frank Samuelson are the first to row across the Atlantic, arriving in Le Havre after leaving Manhattan on June 6
Diamond workers in Amsterdam strike
Tommy Corcoran sets an MLB record for shortstops by recording 14 assists in Cincinnati's 4-2 win against the St. Louis Cardinals
Train derails on a bridge in Eden, Colorado, due to a flash flood, killing 96 people

The first train to travel the length of New Zealand's North Island main trunk line, the "Parliament Special," leaves Wellington for Auckland to greet the US Navy's "Great White Fleet"; passengers include Prime Minister Sir Joseph Ward and members of Parliament, and the trip takes 20.5 hours
Alice Ramsey (22) and three friends become the first women to complete a transcontinental automobile trip
Battle of Mulhouse: First attack on Germany by French troops under General Bonneau on Germany in attempt to retake Alsace
Engagement between British cruiser HMS Gloucester and German cruisers SMS Breslau and SMS Goeben off Greece
French government awards King Albert of Belgium the Great Cross
German army occupies city of Liege, Belgium
Russia invades East Prussia
Dario Resta, driving a Peugeot, wins the first Champ Car race to average over 100 mph at a 100-mile race in Chicago with an average speed of 101.8 mph
WWI: Assault against Russell's Top at Gallipolis, Turkey, killing 232 Australians
League of Nations advises against the Turkish/Iraqi division of the Mosul region
Peace Bridge over the Niagara River between the US and Canada is dedicated to commemorate 100 years of peace between the two countries
US rum smuggler Horace Alderman kills three people
Charles Ruijs de Beerenbrouck forms Dutch government
A large mob estimated at 2,000 people lynches two young Black men, Thomas Shipp and Abram Smith, in Marion, Indiana
Iraqi government forces slaughter over 3,000 Assyrians in the village of Simele in northern Iraq, commemorated as Assyrian Martyrs Day
60% of voters agree to Nazism in Danzig (Gdańsk)
A Finnish quinella in the 5,000 m final at the Berlin Olympics: Gunnar Höckert runs an Olympic record to beat teammate Lauri Lehtinen
Argentina wins the polo gold medal, beating Great Britain 11-0 in the final at the Berlin Olympics, the last time the sport is contested at the Olympics
Nazis close the theological department of Innsbruck University
Alsace-Lorraine is annexed by Nazi Germany during World War II
Churchill recognizes De Gaulle's French government in exile
Largest amount paid for a stamp: $9.48 million for a 1856 British Guiana
551 Jews are shot in the Chișinău ghetto in Romania
Dutch resistance bombs Rotterdam railway
First American offensive in Pacific in WW2, Guadalcanal, Solomon Island
Transport 16 departs with French Jews to Nazi Germany
Red Army recaptures Bogodukhov
Anton de Kom is arrested as a Surinamese resistance fighter
IBM dedicates the first program-controlled calculator, the Automatic Sequence Controlled Calculator (known best as the Harvard Mark I)
Trial for surviving "20 July Plot" (attempted assassination of Adolf Hitler) participants begins in the courtroom of Nazi Judge Roland Freisler in Berlin, Germany
WWII: German counterattack at Avranches in France fails
WWII: US Third Army reaches the suburbs of Brest, Brittany
The Bombay Municipal Corporation formally takes over the Bombay Electric Supply and Transport (BEST)
American Alice Coachman becomes the first Black woman from any country to win an Olympic gold medal when she wins the high jump at the London Games
American swimmer Jimmy McLane wins the 1,500 m for his second gold medal at the London Olympics (4 x 200 m freestyle relay) a US clean sweep of every men's swimming gold medal at the Games
Delfo Cabrera of Argentina wins a dramatic marathon in 2:34:51.6 at the London Olympics
Fanny Blankers-Koen of the Netherlands wins her fourth track and field gold medal at the London Olympics as part of the Dutch 4 x 100 m relay team in 47.5 seconds
First Dutch government of Beel resigns
Swedish race walker John Mikaelsson wins the gold medal in the 10 km event by 30.6 seconds from teammate Ingemar Johansson at the London Olympics
Police bar white players Lou Chirban, Stan Mierko, and Frank Dyle from playing in the Negro League
Douglas D-558-II Skyrocket reaches 1,992 km/h (1,237.77 mph)
The US Viking rocket reaches a height of 210 km, setting a record
Eastern Airlines enters the jet age, using the Electra prop-jet
Charles Mahoney becomes the first Black American to serve as a full UN delegate
Bar-Ilan University is founded in Israel
KSTF TV channel 10 in Scottsbluff-Gering, NE (CBS/NBC) begins broadcasting
Tokyo Telecommunications Engineering, the precursor to Sony, begins selling its first transistor radios in Japan
British government sends three aircraft carriers to Egypt
Dynamite transport explodes in Colombia, killing about 1,200 people
US Congress passes the Civil Rights Act of 1957
US performs a nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Explorer 6 transmits the first TV photo of Earth from space
Côte d'Ivoire (Ivory Coast) gains independence from France
Students stage kneel-in demonstrations in Atlanta churches
Cosmonaut Gherman Titov circles Earth for a full day in Vostok 2
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
31st NFL Chicago College All-Star Game at Soldier Field: Chicago 28, All-Stars 17 (65,000 attendees)
Turkey begins an air attack on Greek Cypriots
Race riot in Lansing, Michigan
First all-computer chess championship is conducted in New York and won by CHESS 3.0 (CDC 6400), a program written by Slate, Atkin, and Gorlen at Northwestern University
Shootout at Marin Courthouse in San Rafael, California, kills four, including the presiding judge
WDHN TV channel 18 in Dothan, AL (ABC) begins broadcasting
A Catholic man is shot dead by a British soldier in Belfast
Apollo 15 returns to Earth
French Philippe Petit walks a tightrope strung between the Twin Towers of the World Trade Center in New York City at a height of 1,350 feet (411.5 meters)
Scientists in Pasadena, California, announce Viking I found strongest indications to date of possible life on Mars
Viking 2 enters Martian orbit after an 11-month flight from Earth
Mount Usu volcano in Hokkaido, Japan erupts for the first time in over 30 years
Eddie Mathews, Addie Joss, and Larry MacPhail are inducted into Baseball Hall of Fame, Cooperstown, New York
"The Washington Star" ceases all operations after 128 years of publication
First Athletics World Championships open in Helsinki, Finland
Some 675,000 AT&T employees strike
Japan beats US, 6-3 in the final of the Los Angeles Olympic baseball demonstration event
The US collects its first Olympic gold medal in women's basketball history with a 85-55 win over South Korea in the final at the LA Games
Takao Doi, Mamoru Mohri, and Chiaki Mukai are chosen to be Japan's first astronauts
Dan Buettner, Bret Anderson, Martin Engel, and Anne Knabe begin a cycling journey of 15,266 miles from Prudhoe Bay, Alaska, to Argentina
Five Central American presidents sign peace accord in Guatemala
Javed Miandadbecomes the first to score 6,000 Test runs for Pakistan as he scores 260 in the drawn fifth Test against England at The Oval
Lynne Cox becomes the first woman to swim from the US to the Soviet Union, 4.3 km across the Bering Sea in water temperatures averaging 43-44°F (6-7°C)
Future Hall of Fame jockey Eddie Delahoussaye wins his 4,000th career race aboard 2-year-old filly Fawn and Hahn in the fourth race at Del Mar
Riots in New York City's Tompkins Square Park
Writers Guild of America ends its six-month strike
U.S. Congressman Mickey Leland (D-TX) and 15 others die in a plane crash in Ethiopia
Manhattan Cable's final day of amnesty to return illegal cable boxes
San Francisco Giants announce the sale of the MLB franchise to a group of Tampa Bay investors, but the move is blocked by other investors
Taking advantage of six months of training on their hometown Terrassa pitch, Spain wins its first hockey gold medal by beating Germany 2-1 in the women's final at the Barcelona Olympics
Tropical storm Brett ravages Venezuela, killing 118 people
Ernesto Samper is sworn in as president of Colombia
First telephone link between Israel and Jordan
STS-85 (Discovery 23) launches into orbit
Ung Huot is appointed Cambodia's first premier
In one of cricket history's closest Tests, and despite a brave unbeaten 43 from Australian tail-ender Brett Lee, England sneaks a 2-run win in the series-turning 2nd Test at Edgbaston
Russo-Georgian War: Georgia moves troops into the Russian-supported self-proclaimed republic of South Ossetia in response to aggression; first European war of the 21st century
Hampered by a back injury, Indian cricket batsman VVS Laxman scores a brave, unbeaten 103 to guide India to a five-wicket win against Sri Lanka in Colombo, drawing the series
200,000 people are evacuated from Shanghai in anticipation of Typhoon Haikui
21 people are killed by a gun attack in a church in Okene, Nigeria
Heavy rain forces 20,000 people to flee their homes in Manila, Philippines
Eleven people are killed by a bombing in Karachi, Pakistan
Sarah Sjöström of Sweden breaks the world record with a time of 55.48 to win the gold medal in the women's 100 m butterfly at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics
England breaks a 19-year drought in a home Test cricket series against South Africa, beating the Proteas by 177 runs in the 4th Test at Old Trafford; Moeen Ali scores 75 not out and takes 5/69
Australia's population reaches 25 million according to the Australian Bureau of Statistics
CW Network announces that Ruby Rose is cast as the lesbian superhero Batwoman
Mendocino Fire becomes the largest recorded fire in California's history at 290,600 acres, overtaking the 2017 Thomas Fire
Swedish furniture brand Ikea opens its first store in India in Hyderabad
Bodies of two teenagers at the center of a huge manhunt and suspected of killing three people on remote British Columbia roads are found after apparent suicides
Largest single-state immigration raid in Mississippi as nearly 700 people are arrested
Wanda Vázquez becomes Puerto Rico's third governor in a week after her predecessor Pedro Pierluisi is removed by the Supreme Court
As part of the winning American 4 × 400 m relay team in Tokyo, Allyson Felix takes her total to 11 Olympic medals and becomes the most decorated female Olympian in track and field history
Dutch athlete Sifan Hassan wins the 10,000 m gold medal in 29:55.32 at the Tokyo Olympics to claim the 5/10 km double
Indian javelin thrower Neeraj Chopra makes history by winning his country's first-ever Olympic track and field gold with a throw of 87.58 m in Tokyo
Ceasefire comes into effect in Gaza after 43 people are killed in three days of violence between the Israeli military and Palestinian Islamic Jihad militants
US Senate passes sweeping Inflation Reduction Act, an economic package designed to combat climate change, address health care costs and tax large corporations
MLB Philadelphia Phillies slugger Kyle Schwarber ties career highs with three home runs and seven RBIs in a 9-4 win over the Dodgers in Los Angeles
Panipak Wongpattanakit of Thailand successfully defends her Olympic 49 kg title, defeating Guo Qing of China 6-3, 2-3, 6-2 in the final at the Paris Games
Thailand's Constitutional Court outlaws the Reformist Party, barring it from forming a government in 2023, along with politicians including Pita Limjaroenrat
Thousands of peaceful counter-protesters hold rallies around England in response to the previous week's violent anti-immigrant protests
WHO confirms a deadlier strain of the mpox virus (formerly Monkeypox) has spread to four neighboring countries of the Democratic Republic of Congo: Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, and Kenya
Battle of Guinegate: Burgundians led by Archduke of AustriaMaximilian Idefeat the army of King of FranceLouis XI
Henry Tudor's (Henry VIIto be) army lands in Milford Haven, South Wales
Francis Drake's fleet returns to Plymouth after a year spent raiding for Spanish treasure
Possible first performance ofShakespeare's tragedy "Macbeth" takes place in the Great Hall at Hampton Court Palace forKing James I
AstronomerJohannes Kepler's is mother arrested for witchcraft
Battle at Ponts-the-Ce, Poitou: French kingLouis XIIIdefeats his mother Marie de Medici

Commander of the Continental Army,George Washington, creates two Honorary Badges of Distinction and a Badge of Military Merit (now the Purple Heart). First time military awards are presented to common soldiers.
Napoleonorders the reinstatement of slavery in Saint-Domingue, Haiti
Pope Pius VIIreinstates the Society of Jesus (Jesuits)
MLB Washington Senators' legendary pitcherWalter Johnsonwins the first of his 417 career games in a 7-2 victory against Cleveland
Progressive (Bull Moose) Party nominatesTheodore Rooseveltfor US President
Dutch cyclistPiet Moeskopswins the first of four consecutive and five total world sprint championships when he beats defending champion Bob Spears of Australia in Copenhagen
NY Yankees sluggerBabe Ruthties MLB record by hitting grand slams in consecutive games for the second time in a 13-1 win against the Philadelphia Athletics
Canadian Prime MinisterMackenzie King's Liberal Party loses the election toRichard Bedford Bennettof the Conservative Party
US Court of Appeals upholds lower court ruling striking down government's attempt to ban controversialJames Joycenovel "Ulysses"
Legendary shortstopLeo Durocherhits the 2,000th Brooklyn Dodgers home run off Reds pitcher Peaches Davis in the eighth inning of a 6-3 victory
MillionaireHoward Hughesis presented with a Congressional Gold Medal
First US commemorative coin featuring an African American,Booker T. Washington(half dollar)
Thor Heyerdahland the crew of the Kon-Tiki crash into a reef in the Tuamotu Islands, French Polynesia, after 101 days crossing the Pacific Ocean
EnglishmanRoger Bannisterbeats Australia's John Landy in the mile at the Empire Games in Vancouver, the first time two men run a sub-4-minute mile in the same race
Boston Red Sox fine sluggerTed Williams$5,000 for spitting at heckling Boston fans in the third incident in three weeks
Soviet premierKhrushchevpredicts USSR economy will surpass US
Jacqueline Kennedybecomes the first presiding US First Lady to give birth (Patrick Kennedy) since Frances Cleveland in 1895; the infant dies three days later

US Congress approves the Gulf of Tonkin Resolution, granting US PresidentLyndon Johnsonthe authority to assist any Southeast Asian nation facing "communist aggression"; he uses it to openly bring the US into the Vietnam conflict
Lee Kuan Yew, Prime Minister of Singapore, signs a separation agreement with Malaysia after two years of political union
Fantasy Records releases "Green River", the third studio album by the American rock bandCreedence Clearwater Revival, featuring the title track and "Bad Moon Rising", and "Lodi"
British keyboard playing singer-songwriterChristine McVie(née Perfect) joins rock band Fleetwood Mac
Yogi Berra,Sandy Koufax,Lefty Gomez,Early Wynn,Josh Gibson, Will Harridge, Buck Leonard, and Ross Youngs are inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame
Thousands of mourners file past the body of PopePaul VI
John LennonandYoko Onobegin recording sessions for their "Double Fantasy" album at the Hit Factory, NYC
Grete Waitzof Norway wins inaugural women's marathon at the first World Athletics Championships in Helsinki, Finland
David Rabe's play "Hurlyburly" premieres on Broadway in NYC directed byMike Nichols, starringWilliam HurtandSigourney Weaver

A delegation of the South African Council of Churches meets with PresidentP. W. Botha, following calls by the church for urgent discussions on the causes of unrest, forced removals and the emergency regulations in the country
Barbra Streisandrecords "The Broadway Album"
American team ofCarl Lewis, Mike Marsh, Leroy Burrell, and Dennis Mitchell sets a 4 x 100 m relay world record of 37.67 seconds in Zürich, Switzerland
Courts rulesManuel Noriegamay access some secret US documents
The Orlando Magic signs NBA No. 1 draft pickShaquille O'Neal
Garth Brooksperforms a free concert in NYC's Central Park for HBO, later releasing it as Garth: Live from Central Park
Tampa Bay third basemanWade Boggsbecomes the first MLB player to hit a home run for his 3,000th hit in the Devil Rays' 15-10 loss to the Cleveland Indians
Democratic presidential candidateAl Gorepicks Connecticut SenatorJoe Liebermanas his running mate, making him the first Jewish person to run for the nation's second-highest office
Los Angeles Lakers announce the retirement of former star player and Executive Vice President of Basketball OperationsJerry Westand name Mitch Kupchak to succeed him
Cubs pitcherGreg Madduxenters the history books with his 300th career win in Chicago's 8-4 triumph over San Francisco at SBC Park
"Eclipse," the third book inStephenie Meyer's "Twilight Saga," is published by Little, Brown with an initial print run of 1 million copies
San Francisco Giants sluggerBarry Bondshits his 756th career home run to break Hank Aaron's long-standing MLB record
Single "Tik Tok" is released byKesha(Billboard Song of the Year 2010)
Jerry Rice,Emmitt Smith, John Randle, Russ Grimm, Rickey Jackson, Floyd Little, and Dick LeBeau are inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame
US Presidential candidateDonald Trumpsays in a CNN interview that news anchor Megyn Kelly had “blood coming out of her eyes, blood coming out of her wherever”
American swimmerKatie Ledeckysets a new world record with a time of 3:56.46 to win the gold medal in the women's 400 m freestyle at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics
British swimmerAdam Peatysets a world record of 57.13 to win the gold medal in the men's 100 m breaststroke at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics
Miami Marlins veteran Japanese outfielderIchiro Suzukibecomes the 30th member of the 3,000-hit club in a 10-7 win against the Rockies
United States men's 4 × 100 m freestyle relay team wins the final in 3:09.92 at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, earningMichael Phelpshis record 19th Olympic gold medal
"Crazy Rich Asians," the first Hollywood film with an all-Asian cast, starringConstance Wu, Henry Golding, andMichelle Yeoh, premieres in Los Angeles
China bans the release of the Winnie the Pooh movie "Christopher Robin" after the character is used to mock Chinese PresidentXi Jinping
Jason Mrazpartners with Fathom Events for the release of "Jason Mraz - Have It All The Movie"
Cardi Breleases the single "WAP," featuringMegan Thee Stallion, which debuts at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100
Kevin Durant, with 29 points, leads the USA to his third and the team's fourth consecutive Olympic men's basketball gold medal with an 87-82 win over France in Tokyo
Brandon Sanderson's Kickstarter for the "Cosmere RPG" game becomes the most successfully funded game in Kickstarter history, raising $41.7 million
ThreeTaylor Swift"Eras Tour" concerts in Vienna, Austria, are canceled after authorities uncover a planned terrorist attack and arrest three people
Construction begins on the dome of Florence Cathedral, designed by Renaissance architect Filippo Brunelleschi
English set alight eight fireships with pitch, brimstone, gunpowder, and tar and cast them downwind towards the closely anchored vessels of the Spanish Armada, scattering the Armada
US deploys troops to Saudi Arabia, beginning Operation Desert Shield
Emperor Gaozu of Tang, Chinese emperor and founder of the Tang Dynasty (618-626), born in Chang'an, Northern Zhou (d. 635)
Kōgyoku [Saimei], 35th and 37th Empress of Japan (642-5, 655-61) and 2nd woman to rule Japan, born in Japan (d. 661)
Elizabeth of Rhuddlan, youngest daughter of King Edward I and Queen Eleanor of Castile, born in Rhuddlan Castle, Denbighshire (d. 1316)
Alonso de Ercilla y Zúñiga, Basque soldier and poet, born in Madrid (d. 1595)
Thomas Lupo, British viol player and composer, baptized in London (d. c. 1627)
Robert Dudley, English explorer and writer (Arcano del Mare), born in Richmond Palace, Surrey (d. 1649)
Georg Stiernhielm, Swedish scholar and poet often known as "father of Swedish poetry" (Hercules), born in Vika, Sweden (d. 1672)
James Bowdoin, American Revolutionary leader and politician, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1790)
Daniel Albert Wyttenbach, German-Swiss classical scholar and historian, born in Bern, Switzerland (d. 1820)
Wilhelmina of Prussia, Princess of Orange (1767-1806), born in Berlin (d. 1820)
Carl Ritter, German geographer and co-founder of modern science of geography, born in Quedlinburg, Prussia (d. 1859)
Louis de Freycinet, French explorer and navigator (published the first map to show a full outline of the coastline of Australia in 1811), born in Montélimar, Drôme (d. 1842)
John Heathcoat, English inventor of lace-making machinery, born in Duffield, Derbyshire (d. 1861)
Princess Amelia, youngest child of King George III of United Kingdom, born in Royal Lodge, Windsor (d. 1810)
Henry Litolff, French composer and pianist, born in London, England (d. 1891)
Faustina Hasse Hodges, English-American composer, born in Malmesbury, England (d. 1895)
August Ahlqvist, Finnish poet (Suomalainen Runousoppi), born in Kuopio, Finland (d. 1889)

Evander M. Law, American Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Darlington, South Carolina (d. 1920)
Auguste Michel-Lévy, French geologist and petrologist, born in Paris (d. 1911)

Andries Cornelis Dirk de Graeff, Dutch politician (Governor-General of Dutch East Indies 1926-31), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1957)
William Auchterlonie, Scottish golfer (British Open 1893), born in St. Andrews, Scotland (d. 1963)
Ulrich Salchow, Swedish figure skater (Olympic gold men's singles 1908; 10 x World C'ship gold), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1949)
Johannes Kotze, South African cricket fast bowler (3 Tests, 6 wickets, BB 3/64; London County, Transvaal, Western Province), born in Cape Town, South Africa (d. 1931)
Ernst Laqueur, Dutch chemist and psychologist (sexual hormones), born near Breslau, Germany (d. 1947)
Joachim Ringelnatz [Hans Bötticher], German writer and artist, born in Wurzen, Saxony (d. 1934)
(Mary) "Billie" Burke, American radio, stage, silent and sound film actress (Zieigfield Follies; The Wizard of Oz; Merrily We Live), born in Washington, D.C. (d. 1970)
Gordon Harker, British actor (Facts of Love, Champagne, Inspector Hornleigh), born in London, England (d. 1967)
Bill McKechnie, American Baseball HOF manager (World Series 1925 Pittsburgh Pirates, 1940 Cincinnati Reds) and coach (World Series 1948 Cleveland Indians), born in Wilkinsburg, Pennsylvania (d. 1965)

(Charles Luckyth) "Luckey" Roberts, stride pianist and composer, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1968) [various sources dispute year - 1887 to 1895][1]
Henny Eman, Aruban politician who founded the Aruban People's Party, born in Aruba (d. 1957)
Franz Johannes Weinrich [Heinrich Lerse], German writer (Himmlisches), born in Hanover, Germany (d. 1978)
Renie Riano, British-American vaudeville and screen actress (Jiggs and Maggie films; The Family Jewels), born in London, England (d. 1971)

Rudolf Ising, American cartoonist (Looney Tunes; Merrie Melodies), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1992)

Clark Poling, American US Army chaplain and Protestant minister (The Four Chaplains), born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 1943)[1]

George Van Eps, American seven-string jazz and session guitarist, born in Plainfield, New Jersey (d. 1998)
Ted Moore, South African-British cinematographer (James Bond), born in Western Cape, South Africa (d. 1987)
C. Buddingh', Dutch poet, born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1985)
John Hogan, American chemist (discovered methods of producing polypropylene), born in Lowes, Kentucky (d. 2012)
Karel Husa, Czech-American Pulitzer prize winning composer (Trojan Women), born in Prague, Czechoslovakia (d. 2016)
Manitas de Plata [Ricardo Baliardo], French gypsy flamenco guitarist, born in Sète, France (d. 2014)
Kenneth Kendall, British broadcaster (BBC News, Treasure Hunt), born in British India (d. 2012)
Art Laboe [Egnoian], American radio disc jockey ("Oldies but Goodies"), songwriter, record producer, and radio station owner, born in Murray, Utah (d. 2022)[1]
Felice Bryant [Matilda Scaduto], American songwriter, with husband Boudleaux ("Bye Bye Love"; "Wake Up Little Susie"), born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 2003)
Julián Orbón (de Soto), Cuban composer, and educator, born in Aviles, Spain (d. 1991)
M. S. Swaminathan, Indian agricultural scientist, leader of the green revolution in India, born in Kumbakonam, Madras Presidency (d. 2023)
Amo Houghton, American politician (Rep-R-New York), born in Corning, New York (d. 2020)
John Woodcock, English cricket journalist ('The Times' 1954-88; Wisden Cricketers' Almanack 1981-86; President Cricket Writers' Club 1986-2004), born in Longparish, England (d. 2021)

Edwin Edwards, American politician (Governor of Louisiana 1992-96), born in Marksville, Louisiana (d. 2021)
Herb Reed, American musician and vocalist (The Platters - "Twilight Time"; "Only You"; "The Great Pretender"), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 2012)
Herbert H. Bateman, American politician (Rep-R-Virginia), born in Elizabeth City, North Carolina (d. 2000)
James Randi, Canadian skeptic magician (Nova), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 2020)
Romeo Muller, American screenwriter, born in the Bronx, New York (d. 1992)
Dick Schulze, American politician (US Rep-R-PA, 1975-93), and businessman, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Don Larsen, AmericanMLBpitcher who hit the only perfect game in World Series history in Game 5, 1956 (World Series MVP 1956; NY Yankees), born in Michigan City, Indiana (d. 2020)
James Pilditch, British designer, born in Buckinghamshire, United Kingdom (d. 1995)
Jo Baer [Josephine Kleinberg], American painter and minimalist artist, born in Seattle, Washington (d. 2025)[1]
Ruth Carter-Stapleton, American evangelist, born in Plains, Georgia (d. 1983)
Veljo Tormis, Estonian composer, specializing in choral music (Curse Upon Iron (Raua needmine)), born in Kuusalu, Estonia (d. 2017)
Charles E. Rice, American legal scholar and author, born in New York (d. 2015)
Bubba Cascio, American race horse trainer (All American Futurity 1968, 70), born in Houston, Texas (d. 2022)
Edward Hardwicke, British actor (Sherlock Holmes), born in London, England (d. 2011)
Jenny Craig, American businesswoman, co-founder of weight loss company Jenny Craig, Inc., born in Berwick, Louisiana
Maurice Rabb Jr., American ophthalmologist, born in Shelbyville, Kentucky (d. 2005)
Jerry Pournelle, American polymath and sci-fi author (Mercenary, Red Dragon), born in Shreveport, Louisiana (d. 2017)
Rahsaan Roland Kirk, American jazz multi-instrumentalist (Bright Moments; The Case of the 3 Sided Dream in Audio Color), born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 1977)
Charles Pope, American soul singer (The Tams - "Hey Girl Don't Bother Me"), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 2013)
Richard L. Tierney, American sci-fi author (Winds of Zarr), born in Spencer, Iowa (d. 2022)
Don McKay, New Zealand rugby union winger (5 Tests; Auckland RU), born in Auckland, New Zealand (d. 2024)
Don Wilson, English cricket fast bowler (6 Tests, 11 wickets; Yorkshire CCC), born in Settle, England (d. 2012)
George Bohanon, American session and jazz trombonist (Motown; Chico Hamilton Quintet), born in Detroit, Michigan
